active_paradigms.idea: Replace power-law x1 scaling with linear x1 terms inside the exponent for numerical stability
active_paradigms.description: The current model uses x1^c which can be unstable when x1 is near 0 or when x1 values span orders of magnitude. Replace with linear x1 modulation inside the exponent: rate = c0 + c1*x1, half_time = c2 + c3*x1, giving sigmoid argument (c0 + c1*x1)*(x0 - c2 - c3*x1). This is a standard Hill-type kinetics parameterization that is globally defined and numerically stable. Keep the lag factor (1-exp(-c6*x0)) for nucleation suppression. The amplitude and baseline remain c4 and c5. This gives 7 constants with better conditioning for least-squares fitting, especially on datasets where x1 is sequential index (1,2,3,...) rather than physical concentration.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Robustness across all 60 datasets, especially those with non-physical x1 values (sequential indices), reducing failures that drive combined_score to 0
active_paradigms.cautions: Ensure initial values are physically reasonable: c0~0.3 (rate), c1~0.1 (x1 modulation), c2~10 (half-time), c3~-1 (half-time decreases with concentration), c4~1.0 (plateau), c5~0.0 (baseline), c6~0.1 (lag rate). The linear form avoids singularities at x1=0.

active_paradigms.idea: Use a Gompertz-type double-exponential with additive x1 influence on growth rate and delay
active_paradigms.description: The Gompertz function y = A * exp(-exp(-k*(t - tm))) is a well-known model for sigmoidal growth with asymmetric lag phase that naturally captures protein aggregation kinetics better than a logistic in many cases. Parameterize as: y = c4 * exp(-exp(-( c0 + c1*x1)*(x0 - c2 - c3*x1))) + c5. This gives an asymmetric sigmoid where the left tail is longer (matching nucleation lag) without needing a separate lag factor. The x1 influence is additive in the exponent argument, making it stable. With 6 constants this is more parsimonious than the current 7-constant model, improving reliability on small datasets. The Gompertz has been specifically validated for amyloid aggregation kinetics in the literature.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Mean NMSE across all datasets by better capturing the asymmetric sigmoid shape of aggregation curves while using fewer constants for more reliable fitting
active_paradigms.cautions: The double-exponential exp(-exp(x)) is bounded in (0,1) which is good for normalized data. Initial values need care: c0~0.3, c1~0.05, c2~10, c3~-0.5, c4~1.0, c5~0.0. The inner exp should not overflow: ensure -(c0+c1*x1)*(x0-c2-c3*x1) stays bounded during fitting.

active_paradigms.idea: Use a 4-parameter logistic with x1-dependent rate and midpoint using tanh for numerical stability
active_paradigms.description: Replace the sigmoid with tanh-based formulation which is more numerically stable in sympy/scipy fitting: y = c4 * (1 + tanh(c0*(x0 - c2) + c1*x1*(x0 - c2) - c3*x1)) / 2 + c5. This is equivalent to a logistic but tanh is often better conditioned numerically. The x1 term modulates both the effective rate (through c1*x1) and the effective midpoint shift (through -c3*x1). This captures how higher concentration shifts the curve left and steepens it. With 6 constants and tanh bounded in (-1,1), predictions are always finite. The (1+tanh(...))/2 maps to (0,1) range matching the rescaled data.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Numerical stability across all datasets, especially edge cases with extreme x1 values or very short/long time series, reducing the number of failed datasets
active_paradigms.cautions: tanh is available as sp.tanh in sympy. Initial values: c0~0.3 (base rate), c1~0.05 (x1 rate modulation), c2~10 (midpoint), c3~1.0 (x1 midpoint shift), c4~1.0 (amplitude), c5~0.0 (baseline). The expression (1+tanh(...))/2 is always in (0,1) so it cannot produce negative predictions or values above 1.
